children.title: Declaration of Policy
children.content: It is hereby declared the policy of the State to promote and enhance economic development through modernization and automation of customs and tariff administration that will efficiently facilitate international trade, prevent and neutralize any form of customs fraud and protect the country’s customs and tariff administration. Towards this end, the State shall:
Develop and implement programs for the continuous enhancement of customs systems and processes that will harmonize customs procedures.
Adopt clear and transparent customs rules, regulations, policies and procedures, consistent with international standards and customs best practices.

Establish a regime of transparency and accountability to customs information, customs laws, rules, regulations, administrative policies, procedures and practices, in order to ensure informed and diligent compliance with customs procedures and procedures by stakeholders.
Consult, coordinate and cooperate with other government agencies and private sector in implementing and developing customs policy.
Provide a fair and expeditious administrative and judicial appellate remedy for customs related grievances and matters.
Employ modern practices in customs administration and utilize information and communications technology in the implementation of customs functions.
Institute professionalism and meritocracy in customs tax administration by attracting and retaining competent and efficient customs officers and personnel to enforce the provisions of this Act.

children.title: Definitions of Terms
children.content: As used in this Act:
Abatement refers to the reduction or elimination, in whole or in part, of duties and taxes where payment has not been made.

Actual or Constructive Exportation refers to the exportation of goods, including the goods to be processed applicable to goods which are being in free circulation, to a foreign customs territory and are intended to remain permanently outside it.
Admission refers to the act of bringing imported goods directly or through transit into a free zone.

Airway Bill (AWB) refers to a transport document for aircraft used by airlines and international freight forwarders which specifically identifies the consignor and consignee who has the right to claim delivery of the goods when they arrive at the port of destination...

Appeal refers to the remedy by which a person who is aggrieved or adversely affected by any action, decision, order, or omission of the Bureau, seeks relief from the Bureau, the Secretary of Finance, or competent court, as the case may be.
Assessment refers to the process of determining the amount of duties and taxes and other charges due on imported and exported goods.

Authorized Economic Operator (AEO) refers to the importer, exporter, customs broker, forwarding agent, legitimate transport provider, and to other entity duly accredited by the Bureau based on the World Customs Organization (WCO) Framework of Standards...
Bill of Lading (B/L) refers to a transport document issued by a shipping line, carrier, and international freight forwarders...
Bureau refers to the Bureau of Customs.
Carrier refers to the person actually transporting goods or in charge of or responsible for the operation of the means of transport...
Clearance refers to the completion of customs and other government formalities necessary to allow goods to enter or for consumption, warehousing, transit, transshipment, or to be exported...
Commission refers to the Tariff Commission.
Conditional Importation refers to the customs procedure under the RKC as a special procedure wherein certain goods can be brought into a customs territory for a specific purpose...
Customs Broker refers to any person who is a bona fide holder of a valid Certificate of Registration issued by the Professional Regulation Commission...
Customs Officer refers to any customs administrative unit that is authorized and accredited to perform all or any of the functions enumerated under customs and tariff laws.
Flagrant Violation refers to any of the following:
Failure to comply with the prohibition against importation of counterfeit goods under a trademark, patent, or copyright law and piracy under Republic Act No. 8293 or intellectual property rights.
Failure to comply with the importation of goods that are the subject of a patent, trademark or copyright litigation.

When the volume of importation is so large, the dutiable value, taxes or duties and the charges due in respect thereof, exceed one hundred million pesos (P100,000,000.00) unless there is a mitigating circumstance...

Smuggling refers to the fraudulent act of importing any goods into the Philippines, or the act of assisting in concealing, buying, selling, disposing, or transporting such goods, knowing them to have been imported contrary to law...
Technical Smuggling refers to the act of importing goods into the country by means of undervaluation, misdeclaration or fraudulent misclassification, including incorrect quantity or weight...

children.title: When Importation Begins and Deemed Terminated
children.content: Importation begins when the goods are brought or introduced into the Philippine territory with the intention to unlade them. Importation is deemed terminated when:
The duties, taxes and other charges due upon the goods have been paid and the goods are free from customs control.
In case the goods are deemed free of duties, taxes and other charges, the goods have legally left the jurisdiction of the Bureau.

children.title: When Duty and Tax are Due on Imported Goods
children.content: Except as otherwise provided for in this Act or in other special laws, duties and taxes are due upon the imported goods at the time of importation, including goods previously exported from the Philippines. Unpaid duties, taxes and other charges, shall incur legal interest of twenty percent (20%) per annum from the date of final assessment under Section 429 of this Act, when such payment becomes due and demandable.
